figures.caption: Figure 7: Summary of 90% prediction intervals with DDH prediction model and a misspecified Cox model for censoring. HAPS uses DDH for the prediction model, Cox for the censoring model; HAPS-A uses XGB to estimate additional nuisance estimators Sk and ξk in the augmentation term.

Figure 8: Mean coverage and median interval length of 80% prediction intervals from HAPS, HAPSDR, and HAPS-A across sample sizes for DGM1–DGM3, across 200 Monte Carlo replications. The dashed horizontal line marks the nominal 80% coverage level.
Figure 11: Summary of 80% prediction intervals across 200 random splits for the PBC data.
Figure 10: Summary of 80% prediction intervals across 200 random splits for the colon cancer data.
Figure 4: Illustration for the data structure and the definitions of Tk and Ck.
Figure 2: Summary of 90% prediction intervals across 200 Monte Carlo replications comparing HAPS with the baseline methods (top) and extensions of HAPS (bottom).

Figure 9: Summary of 90% prediction intervals for HAPS and its two extensions in DGM2 under two DDH prediction model tuning choices. All nuisance models are held fixed: RSF is used to estimate the censoring distribution, and XGB models are used to estimate additional augmentation nuisance parameters Sk and ξk.
Figure 5: Summaries for the 90% prediction intervals in Setup A of the main paper.
Figure 6: Summaries for the 90% prediction intervals in Setup B of the main paper.
Figure 3: Summary of 80% prediction intervals across 200 random splits for data applications.
